summary: The project title explicitly invokes 'Scaling up', which invites a theoretical inquiry into whether performance gains follow a predictable power law or hit a metabolic wall.
body: You propose scaling up acoustic simulation to tame the wilds of speech recognition. This echoes the biological imperative where size dictates function. But in my work on allometric scaling, I found that larger systems don't just grow—they change their efficiency. Does your ASR model exhibit sublinear gains in accuracy relative to compute, like a mammal's metabolic rate, or superlinear innovation like a city? If you simply add more simulation without a law governing the return on investment, you risk a singularity of cost. Can you explain to a bartender why the scaling exponent isn't one?
